intro: Transitioning from TypeScript to SQL shifts a developer's paradigm from writing imperative, statically typed code to constructing declarative, set-based database queries. Instead of allocating memory layouts and handling object properties in an application layer, you instruct a relational engine exactly *what* dataset should be gathered or mutated. This migration replaces transient, compile-time verified models with permanent, disk-backed schemas protected by transaction boundaries.

diffs.from: Static structural type parameters checked ahead of code generation steps, vanishing into raw JavaScript.
diffs.to: Static nominal schemas explicitly defined inside database columns (e.g., INT, TIMESTAMP, VARCHAR), enforced strictly at execution.

diffs.from: C-style procedural layouts combining interfaces, functions, variable closures, and block scopes.
diffs.to: Declarative data clauses structured using unified blocks (SELECT, FROM, JOIN, WHERE) targeting dataset tables.

diffs.from: Short-lived object instances managed inside heap layers using runtime automated garbage collectors.
diffs.to: Persistent database engine caches, index buffers, disk storage allocations, and transaction logs managed by the RDBMS.

diffs.from: Event-driven architecture operating on single threads, scheduling macro task promises continuously.
diffs.to: Synchronous transaction execution models isolated by server connection pools and governed by ACID properties.

diffs.from: Core environment types relying on extensive open NPM registry networks for system utility wrappers.
diffs.to: Specialized built-in mathematical, aggregation, datetime processing, and text matching functions optimized for storage layers.

examples.title: Data Filtering and Cross-Table Merging
examples.description: Contrasting typescript in-memory filtering against an optimized, declarative relational database JOIN statement.
examples.toCode:
SELECT p.title, u.username
FROM posts p
INNER JOIN users u ON p.user_id = u.id
WHERE p.title LIKE 'Release%';

examples.title: Summation and Aggregation Metrics
examples.description: Replacing array loop reduction methods with a native SQL aggregation command.

tips.title: Abandon Client Loops, Rely on Sets
tips.body: Avoid fetching full datasets to filter records in code. Write your logic within SQL WHERE clauses to let the database handle searching efficiently.

tips.title: Sync Database Schema with App Types
tips.body: Use schema generation tools or ORMs (like Prisma or TypeORM) to automatically synchronize your SQL table definitions with your TypeScript interfaces.

tips.title: Index Foreign Keys for Performance
tips.body: Finding items in flat arrays takes O(N) time. Ensure your SQL tables have explicit indexes on foreign keys to maintain fast O(log N) lookup speeds.

tips.title: Use Parameters to Prevent Injection
tips.body: Never insert dynamic strings directly into SQL templates. Always pass variables via parameterized tokens to protect the database against injection attacks.

faqs.question: How are conditional branches written inside declarative SQL files?
faqs.answer: SQL handles conditional branching directly within data streams using the 'CASE WHEN condition THEN outcome ELSE alternative END' clause syntax.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of the TypeScript structural type 'null | undefined' in SQL tables?
faqs.answer: SQL uses a single 'NULL' state to represent missing values, which evaluates under specific three-valued logic rules during data filters.

faqs.question: Can I compose nested array properties within standard SQL data rows?
faqs.answer: Standard relational tables require flat values. However, modern engines like PostgreSQL support JSONB column structures to store and query nested data objects.
